title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the Augmented Human Development Index on the vertical axis and GDP per capita on the horizontal axis for the year 2020. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 0.8, while the horizontal axis displays income in international dollars at 2011 prices on a logarithmic scale spanning from $1,000 to $100,000. Circles represent individual countries, sized according to population, and are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The data points form a clear upward curve, showing that higher income levels coincide with higher development scores. At the lower end of the distribution, countries such as Burundi, DR Congo, Chad, and Malawi cluster below an index value of 0.2 with incomes under $2,000. Moving toward the middle of the chart, nations like India, Pakistan, and Indonesia occupy the 0.3 to 0.4 range with incomes between $5,000 and $10,000. Further up the curve, Argentina, Chile, and Poland sit between 0.6 and 0.7 on the index. The highest values appear in the top right quadrant, where Switzerland, Japan, and the United States reach index scores above 0.8 with incomes exceeding $50,000. Qatar and Kuwait display high income levels near $100,000 but sit lower on the vertical axis around 0.4 compared to the top European and North American entries. Large circles representing populous nations like China and India are visible in the lower-middle income bands. The visual arrangement confirms a positive association where increases in GDP per capita align with increases in the Augmented Human Development Index across the global sample.
